tests.title: IELTS Academic Reading — Test 05
tests.description: Original full-length Academic Reading test: 3 passages, 40 questions, 60 minutes.

sections.title: Passage 1 — The Keepers of Small Museums
sections.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 1–13.
sections.passage:
The Keepers of Small Museums

¶1 Every country has its great national museums, vast institutions whose famous treasures draw visitors from around the world. Far more numerous, though far less celebrated, are the small local museums: the single room above a library, the converted mill, the cottage stuffed with the relics of a vanished trade. These modest collections rarely hold anything a specialist would call priceless, yet together they preserve something the grand museums cannot, namely the texture of ordinary life in a particular place. They are, in effect, the memory of a community kept on shelves. They survive on the margins of the museum world, rarely reviewed and seldom generously funded, and yet they are frequently the only institutions that remember a particular place at all.

¶2 Such places often owe their existence to a single determined individual. A retired teacher, a local historian or simply someone unwilling to see the past thrown away begins by rescuing objects that others regard as junk: old photographs, farm tools, the ledgers of a closed factory. What starts as a private hoard, assembled in a spare room, may slowly grow into a collection that the wider community comes to value. The story of many a small museum is the story of one person's stubbornness, and its survival can depend uncomfortably on whether a successor with the same devotion can be found. When that devotion is not renewed, even a fine collection can drift quietly towards neglect. The fate of the whole collection, in such cases, is bound up entirely with the energy and the lifespan of a single person.

¶3 The objects these museums hold gain their meaning from exactly the place they occupy. A worn miner's lamp in a national gallery is merely an example of its type; the same lamp in the village where the mine once stood may have belonged to a visitor's grandfather. This rootedness is the small museum's great strength. Visitors are not looking at the heritage of humanity in the abstract but at their own past, and the encounter can be unexpectedly powerful. Children, especially, grasp local history more readily when they can see that it happened where they live, and not only in distant capitals.

¶4 Yet the very features that make these museums special also make them fragile. Most run on tiny budgets and depend almost entirely on volunteers, who may lack formal training in how to store or repair delicate items. Paper yellows, metal rusts and textiles crumble when they are kept in damp rooms without climate control, and a single burst pipe can destroy in a night what took decades to gather. When the founding enthusiast retires or dies, a collection may be left without anyone who knows what its objects are or why they matter, and its quiet decline can pass almost unnoticed. A national gallery that loses a masterpiece makes the headlines; a village museum that loses its founder rarely makes even the local paper.

¶5 Technology has begun to offer a partial defence. Digitising a collection, by photographing objects and recording the stories attached to them, ensures that the knowledge survives even if an item is later lost or a museum closes its doors for good. Online catalogues allow a researcher on another continent to discover that some obscure document exists at all. Some small museums have found new audiences by sharing their oddest exhibits on social media, where a single curious object can reach more people in a day than once passed through the door in a year. The digital copy, of course, is not the thing itself, but it is far better than nothing.

¶6 Money, inevitably, remains the central worry. Public funding for culture tends to flow towards the large and prestigious institutions that attract tourists and headlines, leaving the smallest museums to survive on entrance fees, raffles and the goodwill of their neighbours. Some have responded by working together, sharing expertise and mounting joint exhibitions that none could afford alone. Others have woven themselves into local tourism, offering visitors a reason to linger in a town they might otherwise have driven straight through, and so turning heritage into a modest engine of the local economy.

¶7 It would be easy to dismiss these institutions as amateur and sentimental, and some undoubtedly are. But to lose them would be to lose a particular kind of knowledge that exists nowhere else, the unglamorous detail of how people in one valley actually lived, worked and remembered. National museums tell the story of a nation; small museums tell the thousand smaller stories from which a nation is in truth assembled. In an age of standardised high streets and identical shopping centres, a place that insists on remembering what made it different is performing a quietly valuable service. The smallest museums, in their stubborn particularity, are a modest defence against a creeping sameness.

questions.text: Small local museums usually contain objects that specialists would consider priceless.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says these collections 'rarely hold anything a specialist would call priceless', contradicting the statement.

evidence.quote: rarely hold anything a specialist would call priceless

questions.text: Many small museums began as the private collection of one individual.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says such places 'often owe their existence to a single determined individual' whose private hoard grows into a collection.

evidence.quote: owe their existence to a single determined individual

questions.text: An everyday object can mean more in its local museum than in a national gallery.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 contrasts a lamp that is 'merely an example of its type' in a national gallery with the same lamp having personal meaning locally.

evidence.quote: may have belonged to a visitor's grandfather

questions.text: Volunteers at small museums are generally trained conservation specialists.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 says they 'depend almost entirely on volunteers, who may lack formal training', contradicting the claim that they are trained specialists.

evidence.quote: who may lack formal training in how to store or repair delicate items

questions.text: Most small museums receive more public funding than national institutions.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 says public funding 'tends to flow towards the large and prestigious institutions', leaving small museums with little, so this is false.

evidence.quote: tends to flow towards the large and prestigious institutions

questions.text: Small museums preserve the ______ of ordinary life in a particular place.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says they preserve 'the texture of ordinary life in a particular place'.

evidence.quote: the texture of ordinary life in a particular place

questions.text: The local rootedness of its objects is described as the small museum's great ______.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says 'This rootedness is the small museum's great strength.'

evidence.quote: the small museum's great strength

questions.text: Recording objects and their stories digitally preserves the ______ even if an item is lost.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 says digitising 'ensures that the knowledge survives even if an item is later lost'.

evidence.quote: ensures that the knowledge survives even if an item is later lost

questions.text: Which paragraph explains how digital records can protect a collection's knowledge?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 is about digitising collections so knowledge survives and reaches new audiences.

evidence.quote: Technology has begun to offer a partial defence

questions.text: Which paragraph describes physical threats such as damp and burst pipes?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 lists decay and warns 'a single burst pipe can destroy in a night what took decades to gather'.

evidence.quote: a single burst pipe can destroy in a night

questions.text: Which paragraph contrasts the story of a nation with many smaller local stories?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 says national museums tell a nation's story while 'small museums tell the thousand smaller stories'.

evidence.quote: small museums tell the thousand smaller stories

questions.text: Which paragraph mentions museums cooperating to share costs and expertise?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 says some museums respond 'by working together, sharing expertise and mounting joint exhibitions'.

evidence.quote: working together, sharing expertise and mounting joint exhibitions

questions.text: According to Paragraph 3, which group is said to grasp local history especially readily?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says 'Children, especially, grasp local history more readily' when it happened where they live.

evidence.quote: Children, especially, grasp local history more readily

sections.title: Passage 2 — Cooling the Concrete City
sections.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 14–26.
sections.passage:
Cooling the Concrete City

¶1 On a hot summer night, a city can be several degrees warmer than the countryside that surrounds it. The effect, known to scientists as the urban heat island, arises because the materials of which cities are built, asphalt, brick and concrete, soak up the sun's energy through the day and release it slowly after dark. Add the heat pouring from vehicles, factories and air conditioners, and subtract the cooling greenery that open country enjoys, and the result is a built environment that struggles to cool down. As heatwaves grow more frequent and more severe, this once-obscure phenomenon has become a matter of urgent public health. For most of history the urban heat island was a curiosity for meteorologists; it has lately become a problem for mayors.

¶2 The dangers are easily underestimated. Extreme heat kills more people in many countries than floods or storms, yet it does so quietly, without the dramatic images that prompt action. The victims are disproportionately the old, the ill and the poor, who are least able to escape to cooler places or to afford the electricity that air conditioning demands. A severe heatwave can also overwhelm hospitals and buckle railway lines, and the warmer a city's fabric becomes, the worse each successive heatwave is felt. Designing cities that stay cooler, then, is not a comfort but increasingly a necessity. The quiet nature of the threat is precisely what makes it so easy for governments to neglect until a disaster forces their hand. By the time the bodies are counted, the chance to have prevented them has usually passed.

¶3 The most ancient remedy is also among the most effective: shade. A street lined with mature trees can be markedly cooler than a bare one nearby, partly because the canopy blocks direct sunlight and partly because the trees release water vapour, which cools the surrounding air much as sweat cools the skin. Planting trees is cheap, popular and improves the look of a neighbourhood, yet it is not without complications. Trees take years to grow to a useful size, demand water that a hot city may lack, and can be difficult to fit among the pipes and cables that crowd the modern pavement. A tree, in other words, is a long-term investment that an impatient city does not always have the patience to make. Even so, few other measures deliver as much cooling for so little money over the years.

¶4 Where trees cannot go, the buildings themselves can be enlisted. A dark roof may reach a scorching temperature under the midday sun, while a white or pale one reflects much of that energy back into the sky and stays far cooler, lowering the temperature of the rooms beneath. Painting roofs white is among the cheapest cooling measures available, and some cities have begun to require it on new buildings. A more elaborate version replaces the roof with a layer of growing plants, a so-called green roof, which both insulates the building and absorbs rainwater, though it costs considerably more to install and maintain. The choice between a cheap coat of white paint and an elaborate rooftop garden is, for most councils, ultimately a question of budget.

¶5 Researchers are also rethinking the very materials from which cities are made. Conventional dark asphalt can be replaced with paler, more reflective surfaces that absorb less heat, and engineers are developing concretes and coatings designed to shed warmth rather than store it. Water, too, has a part to play: fountains, ponds and even fine mists lower the temperature of the air around them, which is why traditional hot-climate cities so often placed water at their heart. None of these measures is dramatic on its own, but combined across a whole district they can take the sharpest edge off the heat. The art lies in combining many small interventions so that their effects add up across a street, a district and finally a whole city.

¶6 Crucially, the benefits of all this are not shared equally, and that fact has begun to shape policy. Studies in many cities have found that wealthier neighbourhoods are noticeably greener and therefore cooler, while poorer districts, with less tree cover and more bare concrete, bake. Since the residents of those poorer areas are also the least able to cope with heat, the urban heat island quietly deepens existing inequalities. Recognising this, a number of city governments now direct their tree-planting and cooling programmes first towards the hottest and most disadvantaged neighbourhoods, treating shade itself as a question of fairness. Where a tree is planted, it turns out, is never a purely technical decision.

¶7 No single measure will tame the urban heat island, and none can substitute for slowing climate change itself. What the growing toolkit offers is adaptation: a way of making cities bearable, and survivable, through the hotter decades that are now unavoidable whatever happens to global emissions. The encouraging news is that the same steps which cool a city tend to improve it in other ways, cleaning its air, sheltering its wildlife and making its streets more pleasant to walk. A cooler city, it turns out, is usually a better one to live in, which may prove the strongest argument of all for building it. Few responses to a warming climate offer so many rewards for so little regret. A city that prepares for heat now will be glad of it in the decades to come.

options: Putting roofs and walls to work
Cooling as a matter of fairness

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 2
options: Putting roofs and walls to work
Cooling as a matter of fairness
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 stresses that heat 'kills more people ... than floods or storms, yet it does so quietly'.

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 3
options: Putting roofs and walls to work
Cooling as a matter of fairness
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 presents shade as 'The most ancient remedy', focusing on street trees.

evidence.quote: The most ancient remedy is also among the most effective: shade

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 4
options: Putting roofs and walls to work
Cooling as a matter of fairness
correct_answer: Putting roofs and walls to work
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 turns to buildings — pale roofs and green roofs — saying 'the buildings themselves can be enlisted'.

evidence.quote: the buildings themselves can be enlisted

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 5
options: Putting roofs and walls to work
Cooling as a matter of fairness
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 covers reflective surfaces, new concretes and water — 'rethinking the very materials from which cities are made'.

evidence.quote: rethinking the very materials from which cities are made

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 6
options: Putting roofs and walls to work
Cooling as a matter of fairness
correct_answer: Cooling as a matter of fairness
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 shows benefits 'are not shared equally' and frames shade 'as a question of fairness'.

evidence.quote: treating shade itself as a question of fairness

questions.text: The urban heat island effect is mainly caused by
options: city materials absorbing and releasing the sun's heat
correct_answer: city materials absorbing and releasing the sun's heat
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says it arises because asphalt, brick and concrete 'soak up the sun's energy through the day and release it slowly after dark'.

evidence.quote: soak up the sun's energy through the day and release it slowly after dark

questions.text: According to the passage, the people most at risk from extreme heat are
options: the old, the ill and the poor
correct_answer: the old, the ill and the poor
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says victims are 'disproportionately the old, the ill and the poor'.

evidence.quote: disproportionately the old, the ill and the poor

questions.text: Trees cool the air partly by releasing
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says trees 'release water vapour, which cools the surrounding air much as sweat cools the skin'.

evidence.quote: release water vapour, which cools the surrounding air

questions.text: Why do wealthier neighbourhoods tend to stay cooler?
options: they are greener, with more tree cover
they are closer to the sea
correct_answer: they are greener, with more tree cover
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 says wealthier neighbourhoods are 'noticeably greener and therefore cooler', with more tree cover.

evidence.quote: wealthier neighbourhoods are noticeably greener and therefore cooler

questions.text: Painting a roof ______ reflects much of the sun's energy and keeps rooms cooler.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 says a 'white or pale' roof reflects energy and stays cooler.

evidence.quote: a white or pale one reflects much of that energy back into the sky

questions.text: A roof covered with a layer of growing plants is known as a ______ roof.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 calls a roof replaced with growing plants 'a so-called green roof'.

evidence.quote: a so-called green roof

questions.text: Besides cooling, which TWO further benefits of these measures does the final paragraph mention?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 says the same steps improve a city by 'cleaning its air, sheltering its wildlife and making its streets more pleasant to walk'.

evidence.quote: cleaning its air, sheltering its wildlife

sections.title: Passage 3 — The Economy of Attention
sections.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 27–40.
sections.passage:
The Economy of Attention

¶1 More than half a century ago, before the internet existed, an economist observed that a world rich in information would inevitably be poor in something else. A wealth of information, he wrote, creates a poverty of attention, because the more there is to read, watch and answer, the less of our limited notice each item can command. At the time the remark seemed abstract. Today it reads like a prophecy, for the central struggle of much modern technology is precisely the contest to capture and hold the scarce resource of human attention. Everything we now loosely call the attention economy follows from that single, simple imbalance between infinite content and finite minds. The contest for our notice has, in a few short decades, become one of the defining economic facts of the age.

¶2 The reason is commercial. Many of the services people use most heavily, from social networks to video platforms, are offered without charge, and a business that gives away its product must earn its money elsewhere. It does so by selling its users' attention to advertisers. The longer a person can be kept scrolling, watching or clicking, the more advertisements can be shown and the more the service earns. Under this arrangement, the user is not really the customer at all but the product, and the true customers are the advertisers who pay for access to the user's gaze. Once this is understood, a great deal that otherwise seems puzzling about modern technology suddenly begins to make sense. The apparent generosity of free services rests, in the end, on a quiet transaction the user seldom stops to notice.

¶3 To win that contest, such services are engineered with great care to be difficult to put down. Feeds that never end remove the natural stopping point a finished page once provided; notifications interrupt with a promise of something new; and rewards arrive unpredictably, in the same pattern that makes gambling so compelling. None of this is accidental. Teams of talented designers, drawing on a sophisticated understanding of human psychology, work deliberately to maximise the time users spend, and they test every detail against millions of people to discover what holds attention most tightly. The result is an environment shaped, with something close to industrial precision, to be as hard to leave as it can possibly be made.

¶4 The consequences for the individual are still debated, but several are becoming clear. Constant interruption appears to fragment concentration, making sustained, deep thought harder to achieve; many people report that they can no longer read a long book with the ease they once took for granted. Professor Marta Bishop argues that the deepest cost is not lost time but a diminished capacity for the slow, focused attention on which careful reasoning and genuine creativity both depend. The danger, in her view, is not that people waste a few hours, but that they gradually lose the very ability to concentrate. A muscle that is never exercised, she warns, does not simply rest; in time it wastes away. Whether that fear proves fully justified or not, the trend it describes is hard to ignore.

¶5 Not everyone accepts so gloomy a picture. Dr Owen Castellano points out that every major new medium, from the novel to television, has provoked similar fears, most of which proved exaggerated, and that people are not the passive victims such accounts imply. Many learn to manage these tools, switching off alerts, setting limits and using the same technologies to read, learn and connect in ways earlier generations could not. To treat users as helpless, he argues, both underestimates them and conveniently excuses the companies from any blame. People have always found ways to tame the tools they invent, and there is little reason, in his view, to assume this case will prove any different.

¶6 Both sides, however, tend to agree that the responsibility cannot rest on individuals alone. Expecting a person to resist, by willpower, a system painstakingly designed by experts to defeat that very willpower is, the critics suggest, neither realistic nor fair. Attention, on this view, has become something closer to a public good, like clean air or quiet, that markets left to themselves will tend to pollute. Some have called for rules limiting the most manipulative techniques, much as advertising aimed at children is already restricted in many countries, while others place their hopes in services that charge a fee and so have no need to harvest attention at all. The common thread is a recognition that a problem created by design may also have to be solved by design, and by the rules that govern it. Left entirely unregulated, the critics fear, the contest for attention has only one direction in which to travel.

¶7 What gives the issue its weight is that attention is not merely one resource among many. It is the means by which we learn, form relationships and decide what kind of people to become; in a real sense, we are what we attend to. A society whose members can no longer concentrate may find itself unable to address the slow, complex problems that matter most, distracted by a stream of the urgent and the trivial. The economy of attention, in the end, is not only about money or technology. It is about whether human beings can keep control of their own minds in a world expressly built to capture them. That, far more than any quarrel about hours of screen time, is what is finally at stake. The question is no longer whether technology can seize our attention, but whether we can still decide for ourselves where it ought to go. On the answer to that question a great deal more than mere convenience will depend.

questions.text: The economist quoted predicted that abundant information would reduce attention.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 quotes his view that 'A wealth of information ... creates a poverty of attention'.

evidence.quote: A wealth of information, he wrote, creates a poverty of attention

questions.text: On free platforms, the user is best understood as the paying customer.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says 'the user is not really the customer at all but the product', so this is contradicted.

evidence.quote: the user is not really the customer at all but the product

questions.text: The addictive design of these services happens largely by accident.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 states plainly 'None of this is accidental', so the claim of accident is false.

evidence.quote: None of this is accidental

questions.text: Most users would prefer to pay a fee rather than see advertisements.
questions.explanation: Paid services are mentioned as one option, but the passage never states what most users would prefer, so this is Not Given.

questions.text: The writer thinks individuals alone should be responsible for managing their attention.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 says both sides agree 'the responsibility cannot rest on individuals alone', against the statement.

evidence.quote: the responsibility cannot rest on individuals alone

questions.text: The writer regards attention as central to who we become.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 says attention is how we decide what to become and that 'in a real sense, we are what we attend to'.

evidence.quote: we are what we attend to

questions.text: Free platforms make their money mainly by
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says such a business earns money 'by selling its users' attention to advertisers'.

evidence.quote: selling its users' attention to advertisers

questions.text: Which feature does the passage compare to the mechanism that makes gambling compelling?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says rewards 'arrive unpredictably, in the same pattern that makes gambling so compelling'.

evidence.quote: in the same pattern that makes gambling so compelling

questions.text: Constant interruption is said to make which activity harder?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 says constant interruption fragments concentration, 'making sustained, deep thought harder to achieve'.

evidence.quote: making sustained, deep thought harder to achieve

questions.text: Paragraph 6 compares attention to a public good such as
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 says attention has become 'something closer to a public good, like clean air or quiet'.

evidence.quote: a public good, like clean air or quiet

questions.text: The deepest cost of these technologies is a diminished capacity for slow, focused attention.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4: Bishop argues the deepest cost is 'a diminished capacity for the slow, focused attention'.

evidence.quote: a diminished capacity for the slow, focused attention

questions.text: Every major new medium has provoked similar fears, most of which proved exaggerated.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5: Castellano points out that every new medium 'has provoked similar fears, most of which proved exaggerated'.

evidence.quote: has provoked similar fears, most of which proved exaggerated

questions.text: Treating users as helpless both underestimates them and excuses the companies.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5: Castellano argues treating users as helpless 'both underestimates them and conveniently excuses the companies from any blame'.

evidence.quote: both underestimates them and conveniently excuses the companies

questions.text: What does the writer suggest some restrictions on manipulative design could resemble, citing an existing example?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 compares possible rules to how 'advertising aimed at children is already restricted in many countries'.

evidence.quote: advertising aimed at children is already restricted in many countries
